execute_sql.query:
SELECT tester_id,
       submitted_at,
       TRIM(
         COALESCE(NULLIF(comment_ui, ''), '') ||
         CASE WHEN comment_ui IS NOT NULL AND comment_ui <> '' THEN ' ' ELSE '' END ||
         COALESCE(NULLIF(comment_performance, ''), '') ||
         CASE WHEN comment_performance IS NOT NULL AND comment_performance <> '' THEN ' ' ELSE '' END ||
         COALESCE(NULLIF(comment_bugs, ''), '') ||
         CASE WHEN comment_bugs IS NOT NULL AND comment_bugs <> '' THEN ' ' ELSE '' END ||
         COALESCE(NULLIF(comment_pricing, ''), '') ||
         CASE WHEN comment_pricing IS NOT NULL AND comment_pricing <> '' THEN ' ' ELSE '' END ||
         COALESCE(NULLIF(comment_other, ''), '')
       ) AS combined_comment,
       rating,
       nps_score
FROM beta_tester_feedback
WHERE (comment_ui IS NOT NULL AND comment_ui <> '')
   OR (comment_performance IS NOT NULL AND comment_performance <> '')
   OR (comment_bugs IS NOT NULL AND comment_bugs <> '')
   OR (comment_pricing IS NOT NULL AND comment_pricing <> '')
   OR (comment_other IS NOT NULL AND comment_other <> '')
LIMIT 25;

import pandas as pd

turns.speaker: user
turns.utterance: Before I standardize status, can you flag duplicate book entries by isbn13 or title+author?

execute_python.code:
import pandas as pd

# normalize for matching
work = df.copy()
work['isbn13_norm'] = work.get('isbn13').astype(str).str.replace(r'[^0-9Xx]', '', regex=True).str.upper().replace({'NAN': pd.NA, '': pd.NA})
work['title_norm'] = work.get('title').astype(str).str.strip().str.lower().str.replace(r'\s+', ' ', regex=True)
work['author_norm'] = work.get('author').astype(str).str.strip().str.lower().str.replace(r'\s+', ' ', regex=True)

# duplicates by isbn13 (ignore missing)
dup_isbn = work[work['isbn13_norm'].notna()].duplicated(subset=['isbn13_norm'], keep=False)
by_isbn = df.loc[dup_isbn, ['book_id','title','author','isbn13','status','start_date','finish_date']].sort_values(['isbn13','title'])

# duplicates by title+author
work['ta_key'] = work['title_norm'].fillna('') + '|' + work['author_norm'].fillna('')
dup_ta = work['ta_key'].ne('|') & work.duplicated(subset=['ta_key'], keep=False)
by_ta = df.loc[dup_ta, ['book_id','title','author','isbn13','status','start_date','finish_date']].sort_values(['title','author'])

{'dupes_by_isbn13': by_isbn.head(50), 'dupes_by_title_author': by_ta.head(50), 'counts': {'isbn13_dupe_rows': int(dup_isbn.sum()), 'title_author_dupe_rows': int(dup_ta.sum())}}

import pandas as pd

# ensure ordering for rolling calc
month_order = {
    'Jul': 1, 'Aug': 2, 'Sep': 3, 'Oct': 4, 'Nov': 5, 'Dec': 6,
    'Jan': 7, 'Feb': 8, 'Mar': 9, 'Apr': 10, 'May': 11, 'Jun': 12
}

df = df.copy()

# coerce types
if 'fiscal_month_num' not in df.columns:
    if df['fiscal_month'].dtype == 'O':
        df['fiscal_month_num'] = df['fiscal_month'].map(month_order)
    else:
        df['fiscal_month_num'] = pd.to_numeric(df['fiscal_month'], errors='coerce')

df['amount'] = pd.to_numeric(df['amount'], errors='coerce')

# aggregate to monthly totals (in case table is transaction-level)
monthly = (df.groupby(['fiscal_year', 'fiscal_month_num'], as_index=False)
             .agg(monthly_total=('amount', 'sum'))
             .sort_values(['fiscal_year', 'fiscal_month_num']))

# rolling average within each fiscal year
monthly['monthly_total_3mo_roll'] = (monthly.groupby('fiscal_year')['monthly_total']
                                        .transform(lambda s: s.rolling(3, min_periods=1).mean()))

monthly

execute_python.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# guardrails
if 'clicks' not in df.columns or 'impressions' not in df.columns:
    raise ValueError('Expected clicks and impressions columns.')

# compute row-level CTR safely
impr = pd.to_numeric(df['impressions'], errors='coerce')
clk = pd.to_numeric(df['clicks'], errors='coerce')
df['ctr'] = np.where(impr > 0, clk / impr, np.nan)

# pivot: avg clicks and avg ctr by content_type x platform
p = pd.pivot_table(
    df,
    index='content_type',
    columns='platform',
    values=['clicks', 'ctr'],
    aggfunc='mean'
)

# make the headers easier to read
p.columns = [f"{metric}_avg__{plat}" for metric, plat in p.columns]
result = p.reset_index()
result

WITH item_orders AS (
  SELECT
    sku,
    CASE WHEN sale_type = 'flash_sale' OR discount_pct > 0 THEN 'flash_sale' ELSE 'full_price' END AS pricing_group,
    SUM(quantity) AS units_sold,
    SUM(CASE WHEN returned_flag = 1 THEN quantity ELSE 0 END) AS units_returned
  FROM orders_returns_q1_2026
  GROUP BY 1,2
), item_rates AS (
  SELECT
    sku,
    pricing_group,
    CAST(units_returned AS DOUBLE) / NULLIF(units_sold, 0) AS return_rate
  FROM item_orders
  WHERE units_sold > 0
)
SELECT pricing_group, return_rate
FROM item_rates
WHERE return_rate IS NOT NULL;

scenario: History PhD student standardizes messy archival publication dates for timeline visualization, then rejects a suggested auto-fix approach.

turns.speaker: user
turns.utterance: Can you normalize publication_date to YYYY-MM-DD, it’s like “Jan. 12, 1900” and “1900-01-12”.

execute_python.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

s = df['publication_date'].astype('string')
# common cleanup
s = s.str.replace(r'\bJan\.', 'Jan', regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r'\bFeb\.', 'Feb', regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r'\bMar\.', 'Mar', regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r'\bApr\.', 'Apr', regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r'\bJun\.', 'Jun', regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r'\bJul\.', 'Jul', regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r'\bAug\.', 'Aug', regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r'\bSep\.', 'Sep', regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r'\bSept\.', 'Sep', regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r'\bOct\.', 'Oct', regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r'\bNov\.', 'Nov', regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r'\bDec\.', 'Dec', regex=True)

parsed = pd.to_datetime(s, errors='coerce', infer_datetime_format=True)
# keep blanks as blanks
out = parsed.dt.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')
out = out.where(parsed.notna(), pd.NA)

df['publication_date'] = out

# quick sanity check summary
{'rows': int(len(df)), 'parsed_ok': int(parsed.notna().sum()), 'parsed_fail': int(parsed.isna().sum())}

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# filter window
trend = df.loc[(df['year']>=1995) & (df['year']<=2024), ['year','cpi_inflation_yoy']].copy()
trend = trend.groupby('year', as_index=False)['cpi_inflation_yoy'].mean()
trend = trend.sort_values('year')

# 3-year centered moving average for smoothing
trend['cpi_inflation_yoy_ma3'] = trend['cpi_inflation_yoy'].rolling(window=3, center=True, min_periods=1).mean()
trend

execute_sql.query:
SELECT
  grade_level,
  COUNT(*) AS students,
  SUM(CASE WHEN is_chronically_absent = 1 OR is_chronically_absent = TRUE THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS chronically_absent_students,
  1.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN is_chronically_absent = 1 OR is_chronically_absent = TRUE THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) / COUNT(*) AS chronic_absenteeism_rate
FROM semester_attendance_summary
WHERE semester = (SELECT MAX(semester) FROM semester_attendance_summary)
GROUP BY grade_level
ORDER BY chronic_absenteeism_rate DESC;

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# candidate formula
cand = df['ri_covered_cost_usd'] / df['on_demand_cost_usd']

out['cand_coverage'] = cand
out['abs_diff'] = (out['ri_coverage_rate'] - out['cand_coverage']).abs()

summary = {
    'rows': int(len(out)),
    'nonnull_compared': int(out[['ri_coverage_rate','cand_coverage']].dropna().shape[0]),
    'mean_abs_diff': float(out['abs_diff'].mean(skipna=True)),
    'p95_abs_diff': float(out['abs_diff'].quantile(0.95, interpolation='linear')),
    'max_abs_diff': float(out['abs_diff'].max(skipna=True)),
    'pct_within_0_001': float((out['abs_diff'] <= 0.001).mean(skipna=True)),
    'pct_within_0_01': float((out['abs_diff'] <= 0.01).mean(skipna=True))
}

# show biggest mismatches for inspection

summary, mismatches

execute_python.code:
import pandas as pd

# split resource_meta like 'aws:us-east-1:prod' -> 3 columns
parts = df['resource_meta'].astype('string').str.split(':', n=2, expand=True)
df['cloud_provider'] = parts[0]
df['region'] = parts[1]
df['environment'] = parts[2]

# basic validity flag for policy reporting
bad = df[['cloud_provider','region','environment']].isna().any(axis=1) | (df[['cloud_provider','region','environment']] == '').any(axis=1)
df['resource_meta_valid_3parts'] = ~bad

# grouped compliance table (mean compliance, count)
result = (df[df['resource_meta_valid_3parts']]
          .groupby(['cloud_provider','environment'], dropna=False)
          .agg(resources=('resource_id','count'),
               avg_tagging_compliance_pct=('tagging_compliance_pct','mean'))
          .reset_index()
          .sort_values(['cloud_provider','environment']))
result

turns.speaker: user
turns.utterance: Can you check what’s going on with content_type, it looks messy.
turns.rationale: They might want a quick profile of the column (unique values, nulls) which is descriptive profiling, or they might want a specific SQL-style breakdown/counts of each value which is a query.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.utterance: Do you want a quick profile of that column (nulls, unique values), or a grouped count of each content_type so you can see the top variants?

turns.speaker: user
turns.utterance: Grouped counts please, and normalize stuff like E-book, eBook, guide into a few buckets.

execute_python.code:
import pandas as pd

s = df['content_type'].astype('string').str.strip().str.lower()
# normalize punctuation/spaces
s = s.str.replace(r"[\s_]+", " ", regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r"[^a-z0-9\s-]", "", regex=True)
s = s.str.replace(r"\be\s*-?\s*book\b", "ebook", regex=True)

# bucket common variants
mapped = s.replace({
    'e book': 'ebook',
    'e-book': 'ebook',
    'ebook': 'ebook',
    'white paper': 'whitepaper',
    'whitepaper': 'whitepaper',
    'case study': 'case_study',
    'casestudy': 'case_study',
    'guide': 'guide',
    'playbook': 'guide',
    'webinar': 'webinar'
})

# keep unknowns as-is for review
mapped = mapped.fillna(pd.NA)

out = (
    mapped.value_counts(dropna=False)
    .rename_axis('content_type_normalized')
    .reset_index(name='leads')
)
out

scenario: Program manager checks duplicate participant IDs in a case management export, then wants unduplicated quarterly counts and a saved dedupe preference.

turns.speaker: user
turns.utterance: Before we touch it, how many participant_ids are duplicated in this export?

execute_sql.query:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_rows,
       COUNT(DISTINCT participant_id) AS distinct_participants,
       SUM(CASE WHEN cnt > 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS participant_ids_with_duplicates,
       SUM(CASE WHEN cnt > 1 THEN cnt - 1 ELSE 0 END) AS extra_duplicate_rows
FROM (
  SELECT participant_id, COUNT(*) AS cnt
  FROM case_mgmt_export
  WHERE participant_id IS NOT NULL
  GROUP BY participant_id
) t;

turns.speaker: agent
turns.utterance: Got it. I’ll count how many participant IDs appear more than once and how many extra rows that creates, so you can see the scale of the sync duplicates.

turns.speaker: user
turns.utterance: Also, can you give me unduplicated participant counts by quarter, and save my default to keep the latest service_date when de-duping?
turns.rationale: They’re asking for a new analysis result (unduplicated counts by quarter) and, at the same time, asking to store a personal default rule for future deduping behavior.

execute_sql.query:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT
    participant_id,
    DATE_TRUNC('quarter', service_date) AS service_quarter,
    service_date,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
      PARTITION BY participant_id, DATE_TRUNC('quarter', service_date)
      ORDER BY service_date DESC, last_modified_at DESC
    ) AS rn
  FROM case_mgmt_export
  WHERE participant_id IS NOT NULL
    AND service_date IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT
  service_quarter,
  COUNT(DISTINCT participant_id) AS unduplicated_participants
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
GROUP BY service_quarter
ORDER BY service_quarter;

manage_memory.value: When removing duplicates, keep the record with the most recent service_date (tie-breaker: latest last_modified_at).

scenario: Property manager wants a weekly Monday AM dashboard for occupancy, maintenance tickets, and rent collection across all properties.

execute_sql.query:
WITH latest AS (
  SELECT MAX(week_start) AS week_start
  FROM property_ops_weekly
)
SELECT
  p.week_start,
  ROUND(SUM(p.units_occupied) * 1.0 / NULLIF(SUM(p.units_total), 0), 4) AS occupancy_rate_total,
  SUM(p.open_maintenance_tickets) AS open_maintenance_tickets_total,
  ROUND(SUM(p.rent_collected_usd) * 1.0 / NULLIF(SUM(p.rent_billed_usd), 0), 4) AS rent_collection_rate_total
FROM property_ops_weekly p
JOIN latest l
  ON p.week_start = l.week_start
GROUP BY p.week_start;

scenario: Researcher validates survey Voter_Likelihood responses are integers 1-5, then asks to view distribution and apply suggested fixes.

execute_sql.query:
SELECT Voter_Likelihood, COUNT(*) AS n
FROM survey_responses_2024_wave1
WHERE CAST(Voter_Likelihood AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 1 AND 5
  AND CAST(Voter_Likelihood AS INTEGER) = Voter_Likelihood
GROUP BY Voter_Likelihood
ORDER BY Voter_Likelihood;

scenario: DevOps team appends a new deployment window row, then asks to reshape pipeline metrics and verify a saved failure-rate metric.

turns.speaker: user
turns.utterance: Add a new row for last night’s window, api-service in prod, 42 total deploys.

turns.speaker: agent
turns.utterance: Done, I added that deployment window row for api-service in prod. Want me to sanity-check totals vs successful plus failed?

turns.speaker: user
turns.utterance: Can you pivot this so each row is a window and services are columns, and also pull up deployment_failure_rate to confirm it saved?
turns.rationale: This combines a reshaping request (pivoting the table layout) with an analysis request (retrieving/confirming a metric definition). Since they require different flows, it should be handled as a multi-step outline.

execute_sql.query:
SELECT window_start, window_end, service, environment,
       CASE WHEN total_deploys = 0 OR total_deploys IS NULL THEN NULL
            ELSE (failed_deploys * 1.0) / total_deploys END AS deployment_failure_rate
FROM deployment_pipeline_runs
WHERE environment = 'prod'
ORDER BY window_start DESC
LIMIT 200;
